fund.name: HDFC Flexi Cap Fund - Growth Option - Direct Plan

allocations.rationale: This fund invests across large, mid, and small companies, giving you exposure to the whole market through one fund. At 35%, it forms the core of your portfolio for steady long-term growth.

fund.name: Motilal Oswal Nifty Midcap 150 Index Fund - Direct Plan

allocations.rationale: Mid-cap stocks grow faster than big companies but are riskier. This low-cost index fund tracks the top 150 mid-caps, so you get that growth potential without paying high management fees.

fund.name: Nippon India Small Cap Fund - Direct Plan Growth Plan - Growth Option

allocations.rationale: Small companies can deliver the highest returns over long periods, but they are very volatile. Keeping 10% here adds aggressive growth potential to your portfolio for your long retirement goal.

fund.name: Sundaram Long Term Tax Advantage Fund Series I Direct Plan - Growth

allocations.rationale: Since you are in the new tax regime, ULIPs/ELSS don't give 80C tax benefits anymore, but the 3-year lock-in here still works as a disciplined way to invest in safe tax-efficient units. Plus, these tax-saving funds usually have decent returns.

Gold helps reduce the ups and downs of your stock portfolio. It doesn't go up and down at the same time as stocks. Since you are interested in gold as a hedge, this ETF is a great way to own Indian gold directly.

fund.name: ICICI Prudential Large & Mid Cap Fund - Direct Plan - Growth

allocations.rationale: This gives you exposure to both big and medium-sized companies. It is slightly less risky than small caps and offers stable growth. This makes up the safe-equity part of your investment.

plan.setup_phase:
Step 1: Open a Direct Mutual Fund account on any platform like Groww, Coin by Zerodha, or Kuvera. Do not open a regular account; choose 'Direct' to save on fees (1% vs 0.5%).

Step 2: Link your bank account for auto-debit.

Step 3: Set up SIPs for each fund as per the amounts listed above. You will see 6 SIPs running: HDFC Flexi (₹8,750), Motilal Oswal Midcap (₹3,750), Nippon Small Cap (₹2,500), Sundaram ELSS (₹1,250), UTI Gold (₹2,500), and ICICI Large & Mid (₹6,250).

Step 4: For your children's education goal of ₹20L in 3 years, calculate the monthly SIP needed separately using online SIP calculators. Currently, you already have ₹9.63L, which is your head start. For the next 36 months, let the existing money grow and add a separate SIP of around ₹30,000–₹32,000/month (equity-heavy) to build that specific corpus.
review_checkpoints:
After 6 months: Check that all 6 SIPs are running and debiting correctly from your bank account.
- Year 1: Check if any fund returns have lagged their peers significantly. 
- Year 2: Rebalance the portfolio (see below) and check the ELSS fund - ensure your total investment so far is tracked.
- Every 3 years: Review whether the mid-cap and small-cap allocations have grown too large; if >20%, shift some to large caps to maintain your risk level.
- 3 years before children's education goal (i.e., in 9 years): Shift that portion of the portfolio to safer options to lock in returns.
The 'Rebalancing' feature on your app or the 'manual', where you will check periodically, adjust the portfolio.

Once a year, check if your mix has changed a lot. For example, if your mid-cap and small-cap portion grows, because stocks went up a lot, you will have to move some of the money back to the safer options to get back to the target mix. This keeps your risk under control.
Also, since your children's education fund goal is 3 years away, start shifting that separate corpus (maybe 20% of the ₹9.63L) into a more conservative debt fund around year 2.

base: We estimate that your portfolio will grow at an average rate of around 11-12% per year. The 35% Flexi Cap and 25% Large & Mid are like 11% for flexi and 12% for large & mid, 15% for mid cap, 18% for small cap, 11% for gold (historic), and 9% for the rest (debt), weighted together gives us around 12% (base).
- bull: If the market does very well, we expect higher returns of around 16% (12% + 4%)
- bear: If the market does poorly, we assume you will earn about 8% (12% - 4%)
plan.disclaimer: For research/educational purposes only. Not certified financial advice.

aps.reasoning: The plan shows a mixed profile. Only 14% of allocations (uncommenting about passive) are explicitly passive (one Index fund and one Golt ETF); the rest are active categories (Flexi, Small Cap, ELSS, Large&Mid). Passive equity fraction is ~15% out of the equity sleeve. Turnover is moderate (annual rebalancing and some tactical checks every 3 years), so moderately low but not buy-and-hold. Cost emphasis is moderate (uses Direct plans and mentions SVPs). Research is minimal (no individual stock/fund research described). Time horizon is long-term (retirement focus, but there are short-term 3-year triggers for kids' education). Portfolio activeness is circa moderate (high % of active categories with no risk metrics shown).

pqs.reasoning: The plan scores well on diversification (equity themes, large/mid/small, gold) and internal consistency (clear rationales, rebalancing rules). Goal alignment is good (matches the 19-year horizon and moderate risk appetite). However, the tax efficiency is penalized because the investor is in the 'new_regime' (where 80C is irrelevant), yet the plan recommends an ELSS fund (locking wealth for only tax benefits). Since the investor cannot use 80C, capital could be better deployed in a tax-efficient large-cap or flexi-cap fund instead of a pure tax-saver fund. Additionally, while the plan mentions a separate corpus for the child's education goal, it is somewhat superficially treated, which prevents a perfect goal_alignment score.
